mnemonics.title: Remember Batch Norm Purpose
mnemonics.content: 'Internal Covariate Shift Makes Training Brittle' - BatchNorm reduces this shift, making training stable.

mnemonics.title: Remember He vs Xavier
mnemonics.content: 'He Has Half the signal, XavIER has Equal signal' - He (2/n) accounts for ReLU zeroing half, Xavier (1/n) for tanh/sigmoid.

mnemonics.title: Remember Vanishing Gradient Signs
mnemonics.content: 'Early layers learn slow, loss barely drops' - Key symptom that vanishing gradients are occurring.

mnemonics.title: Remember Residual Connection Formula
mnemonics.content: 'F of x plus x, gradient highway' - Output = F(x) + x creates direct gradient path.

acronyms.expansion: Xavier Glorot Initialization - weights from N(0, 1/sqrt(n_in+n_out))
Kaiming He Initialization - weights from N(0, sqrt(2/n_in))
Adaptive Moment Estimation - combines momentum with adaptive learning rates
Residual Network - uses skip connections for very deep networks
Exponential Linear Unit - f(x) = x if x>0, alpha*(exp(x)-1) if x<=0
acronyms.usage: Alternative to ReLU that doesn't die but is computationally more expensive

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a chain of tiny multipliers - each layer multiplies gradient by 0.25, quickly approaching zero like a fading signal through many filters
Think of a highway alongside local roads - gradient can 'drive straight' through the shortcut rather than taking the 'winding path' through all layers
Picture each layer's inputs as students being 'normalized' to similar height (zero mean, unit variance) before entering the 'classroom' (transformation)
Imagine each layer expecting different 'language' of input - Batch Norm standardizes the language so each layer understands the previous layer's output
Think of feedback loop with microphone - small sound gets amplified repeatedly until it becomes chaotic noise, similar to gradient getting multiplied by large values repeatedly

analogies.analogy: Like whispers passing through many people in a game of 'telephone' - the message (gradient) becomes so faint by the time it reaches the first person (early layer) that they can't hear anything
Like emergency exits in a building - if the regular path (backpropagation through layers) is blocked, there's a direct route to escape (gradient flows directly)
Like a broken light switch stuck in 'off' position - no matter what input (voltage) you give, it always outputs zero and can't be fixed without replacement
Like taking giant leaps across a valley trying to find the bottom - you keep overshooting the minimum and may end up on the other side or even going upward
Like a teacher changing their teaching style mid-course - students (layers) who adapted to the old style struggle when the input distribution suddenly changes
